description: Detects attempts to disable, delete, or modify CloudTrail logging. This is one of the first actions a sophisticated attacker takes after gaining access — if they can blind the audit trail, all subsequent actions go unrecorded. This rule fires on both successful and failed attempts because the intent is malicious regardless of the outcome.
query_kql: eventSource:"cloudtrail.amazonaws.com" AND (eventName:"StopLogging" OR eventName:"DeleteTrail" OR eventName:"UpdateTrail" OR eventName:"PutEventSelectors")

threshold.time_window: N/A — single event triggers alert, including failed attempts

triage_steps: IMMEDIATELY verify that CloudTrail is still logging (aws cloudtrail get-trail-status)
If logging was stopped, re-enable it immediately
Identify the principal who made the call
Check if this correlates with any approved maintenance or IaC deployment
Review ALL actions by this principal in the last 24 hours — assume compromise
Check for other defense evasion indicators (GuardDuty disabled, Config rules deleted)
false_positive_notes: Terraform or CloudFormation may update trail configurations during deployments. Legitimate trail modifications should come from IaC service roles, not human users. PutEventSelectors may fire during routine configuration changes.
tuning_guidance: Exclude known IaC roles from UpdateTrail and PutEventSelectors only. NEVER exclude StopLogging or DeleteTrail from any principal — there is no legitimate reason for a human to stop logging outside of account decommissioning.
response_actions: IMMEDIATELY re-enable CloudTrail if logging was stopped
Disable the principal that made the call
Rotate all credentials for the compromised identity
Review CloudTrail logs from BEFORE the tampering for the full attack timeline
Check S3 access logs on the CloudTrail bucket for log file deletion attempts
